Coffee Wars · Chapter 9

Who Gets the Customer — And Who Keeps Them?

Markov Transition Matrix · Steady-State Distribution · Push–Pull Dynamics · Retention Evolution · 9,757 reviews · 2017–2026

Key Findings — All Five Brands
How to read: Each card shows the most critical finding per brand. The number is the most strategically significant metric — long-run share gain/loss, retention rate, or trajectory. Together these five cards tell who wins, who leaks, and who struggles to convert visits into loyalty.
+2.3 pts
Long-run gain
Current 34.8% → steady state 37.1%. Only brand structurally positioned to grow. Market gravity flows toward Costa.
–3.2 pts
Long-run loss
Despite strong value pull, Greggs donates 40% of switchers to Costa. Steady-state erosion of 3.2 pts projected.
58→16%
Retention collapse
58.3% retention (2017–19) → 15.8% (2022–26). Steepest decline of any brand. Premium pricing under pressure.
13.5%
Retention — trial, not loyalty
Pret attracts inflows but cannot hold them. Ch.7 TWCS: Price & Value and Speed are root causes.
+0.8 pts
Positive net trajectory
Lowest retention (8.2%) yet positive steady-state delta. Scale is the constraint, not experience quality.
9.4 — Markov Brand Transition Matrix
How to read this matrix: Each row = a brand's customers. Percentages show where they go on their next observed store visit. Bold diagonal = same brand again = retention. Off-diagonal = switching to a competitor. Rows sum to 100%. Darker cell background = stronger flow. Verified from 543 multi-brand Trustpilot reviewers (2017–2026).

9.5 — Net Customer Flow Balance
How to read: Incoming = total flow arriving from all other brands. Outgoing = total flow leaving to all competitors. Net balance = incoming minus outgoing. Positive net = brand is a net attractor. Negative = net donor. Note: a smaller brand can have a positive net and still be a minor player — absolute size still matters.

9.6 — Retention: Who Keeps Their Customers?
How to read: Retention = probability a customer chooses the same brand again on their next visit. This is the diagonal of the Markov matrix. High retention = visits convert to repeat behaviour. Low retention = "revolving door" — the brand attracts visitors but fails to keep them. Retention is the most operationally actionable metric in this chapter.
Brand Retention Rates (%) — diagonal of Markov matrix
35.5%
29.3%
17.3%
13.5%
8.2%
9.7 — Steady-State Distribution: Where Is This Market Heading?
How to read: If current transition probabilities remain unchanged, the market converges to a fixed distribution — the steady state — computed from the dominant eigenvector of the transition matrix. Faded bar = current observed share. Solid bar = long-run equilibrium. The delta shows whether a brand is on a ▲ growth or ▼ declining structural trajectory. This analysis is a new contribution not in the original chapter.

▲0.8 pts
9.8 — Ergodicity & Convergence: No Brand Can Lock In Its Customers
How to read: P¹ = next store visit. P² = two visits later. P^n = n visits later. Each P^n row answers: "A customer at Brand X today — where will they be after n visits?" When all rows become identical, starting brand no longer matters — this is convergence. P^n is also a probability distribution over future locations: as n grows it stabilises into the steady state. The Pret→Costa example shows the mechanism concretely: 33.1% at P¹, rising to 37.1% by P⁵ — Costa's gravitational pull is structural.
Full Convergence in 5 Steps
The market is fully ergodic. By the 5th store visit, the matrix P⁵ produces identical rows — meaning where a customer ends up is governed by market structure, not their starting brand.

Strategic implication: No brand has built a loyalty moat. The winner is the brand that makes the next visit more likely — not the one that tries to lock customers in.
Pret → Costa example:
P¹ (next visit): 33.08% chance of being at Costa
P² (2 visits later): 36.78% → rising fast
P³ (3 visits later): 37.07% → nearly converged
P⁵ (5 visits later): 37.08% → stable

Meanwhile Pret → Pret falls: 13.53% → 11.15% → 11.10%
Costa's gravitational pull is structural, not coincidental.
P¹ — Next store visit Each row is different. Starting brand matters.

At P⁵: every row reads 37.1 / 29.6 / 17.5 / 11.1 / 4.7% — regardless of starting brand. P^n is also a probability distribution over future locations: as n grows it stabilises into the steady state, which is both a market forecast and proof no brand permanently owns its customers.
9.9 — Push–Pull Asymmetry: The Costa–Greggs War
How to read: Left panel = where each brand's largest outflow goes (its biggest threat). Right panel = where each brand's largest inflow comes from (its biggest source). Key asymmetry: Greggs sends 40.0% of its switchers to Costa, but Costa sends only 31.5% to Greggs — an 8.5-point gap making Greggs a net donor to Costa in their bilateral relationship.
Biggest Competitive Threat (largest outflow)
→ 31.5%
→ 40.0%
→ 39.6%
→ 33.1%
→ 31.1%
Costa's biggest threat is Greggs (value pressure), not a premium rival — a two-front war.
Biggest Recruitment Source (largest inflow)
→ 40.0%
→ 31.5%
→ 19.7%
→ 12.0%
→ 8.3%
Greggs → Costa: 40.0% vs Costa → Greggs: 31.5%. An 8.5-point asymmetry — Greggs is a net donor to Costa.
9.10 — Retention Evolution: 2017–2026
How to read: Retention tracked across three periods: pre-pandemic (2017–2019), pandemic (2020–2021), post-pandemic (2022–2026). Green ▲ = improved. Red ▼ = declined. Most striking: Starbucks collapsed from 58.3% to 15.8% — the largest structural decline. Costa is the only brand that improved. Dashes = insufficient data.
Brand2017–20192020–2021 (Pandemic)2022–2026Change
Costa35.0%32.1%34.7%▼0.3 pts
Greggs42.9%27.3%30.0%▼12.9 pts
Starbucks58.3%9.5%15.8%▼42.5 pts
Pret A Manger—18.2%11.7%—
Caffe Nero—22.2%5.7%—
9.11 — Customer Satisfaction (ABSA) vs Retention
How to read: Each circle = one brand. Horizontal = average ABSA score (higher/less negative = better experience). Vertical = retention rate. Dashed line = approximate trend. Brands above the trend retain more than their satisfaction score predicts. Brands below under-retain. Caffe Nero is the key outlier — decent ABSA but very low retention, suggesting a scale problem, not an experience problem.
ABSA Score vs Retention Rate — by Brand
Note: Caffe Nero anomaly — reasonable ABSA but very low retention suggests a scale/accessibility problem, not an experience problem.
9.12 — Strategic Conclusion: Three Asymmetries That Define This Market
This chapter's central finding is that the UK coffee market is structurally porous and fully ergodic within 5 brand interactions. No brand has built a durable loyalty moat. Everyone gains customers; everyone loses them. But three asymmetries separate winners from losers.

First: Costa is the market's gravitational centre. Four out of five brands list Costa as their biggest competitive threat. Costa's steady-state share (37.1%) exceeds its current share (34.8%) by 2.3 points — the only brand structurally positioned to grow. Yet this strength is built on centrality, not deep loyalty.

Second: Greggs attracts but cannot hold. Strong value pull — but Greggs is a net donor to Costa (40.0% vs 31.5%). Steady-state share sits 3.2 points below current share, signalling structural erosion if behaviour is unchanged.

Third: Starbucks' premium contract is broken. Retention collapsed from 58.3% (2017–2019) to 15.8% (2022–2026). This is not a cyclical dip — it is a structural reassessment of whether the premium price is worth paying at every visit.

For Pret A Manger: market presence is maintained but retention at 13.5% means the brand functions as a trial destination. Chapter 7's TWCS analysis identifies the operational drivers: Price & Value and Speed dominate the priority index. The strategic question is not whether Pret can attract customers — it demonstrably can — but whether it can close the gap between first visit and second.
